entry.title: SAP BTP - CI - Immutable Routing Principle: in SAP CPI with Value Mapping metadata ODCP in practice
entry.content:
"The artifact is immutable. The metadata is the architecture."

Introduction

If you have ever worked with SAP PI/PO, you know how elegant the IDoc landscape can be. A single tRFC destination. A single IDoc port. All IDocs from all systems flowing through one gateway, with routing handled centrally in the Integration Directory. Adding a new IDoc type meant adding a configuration entry — not creating a new interface.

When teams migrate to SAP BTP Integration Suite (CPI), that elegance often disappears. The common approach creates one iFlow per IDoc type, one endpoint per interface, one SAP port per destination. A landscape with 20 IDoc types across 3 SAP systems quickly becomes 60 iFlows, 60 ports, and 60 deployment cycles. Any new IDoc type or new sender system requires a developer, a change request, and a redeployment. This is what ODCP calls Package Sprawl and Endpoint Chaos.

This post presents a pattern that restores that elegance in CPI — and goes further. It is based on the same architectural principle applied in SAP BTP API Management through the GDCR (Gateway Domain-Centric Routing) and DDCR (Domain Driven-Centric Router) patterns: the routing artifact is immutable. What grows is the metadata.

In API Management, the proxy never changes — the KVM evolves.
In SAP CPI, the iFlow never changes — the Value Mapping evolves.

Same principle. Two layers of the same architecture.

⚠️ everything here should be adopt, this is a basic presentation.

The video:

The main flow to receive all the IDOCS from any company e division: id01.shared.routing.sap.idoc.pd.f.in.async

The second iflow inside each Domain package, in this case Sales O2C: id00.o2c.routing.pd.idoc.iflow.f.in.async

The logic is the same just change the parameters in the groovy script regards the Value Mapping table, there are two, one core, and one inside each package this granulation

⚠️Adaptation Notice

This pattern is designed within the SDIA governance framework using ODCP naming. If your organisation does not follow SDIA/ODCP, adapt the package names, iFlow names, and VM artifact names to your convention. The routing logic and Groovy script work independently of naming.

What is SDIA — and why does it matter for SAP CPI?

SDIA (Semantic Domain Integration Architecture) is a vendor-agnostic governance model built on one invariant: the business domain is the primary key of every artifact, from the API gateway to the data layer. It prevents the three most common failure modes in enterprise integration: Package Sprawl, Naming Chaos, and Credential Blackout.

ODCP (Orchestration Domain-Centric Pattern) is Layer 3 of SDIA and governs SAP BTP Integration Suite directly — one package per domain, every artifact carries the domain token in a deterministic name, credentials segmented by domain so a rotation in Finance never affects Logistics or Sales.

In this pattern, SDIA is the governance boundary. The Receiver Layer is infrastructure — immutable, owned centrally. Once the message crosses into the Domain Layer, it enters SDIA governed space where each domain package is independently owned, deployed, and operated. The Value Mapping is the metadata contract that connects both layers.

Full specifications: SDIA — DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18877635 · ODCP — DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18876593

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ SDIA — IDoc Generic Routing Architecture                             │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

 [ SAP Sender (HTTPS / IDoc SOAP) ]
                │
                ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ LAYER 1: RECEIVER LAYER (Immutable · iFlow: id01.shared.sap.idoc...) │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ 1. Extract XPath ──► IDOCTYP · MESTYP · SNDPOR                        │
│ 2. Composite Key ──► Key: ORDERS05.ORDCHG.SAPPN1                     │
│ 3. VM Lookup     ──► Checked against: vm.shared.idoc.routing         │
└──────────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────┘
                                   │
                         ┌─────────┴─────────┐
                   [ Mapped? ]         [ No ]──► [ Error End ] (HTTP 500)
                         │ Yes                    IDoc status 02
                         ▼
             〔 ProcessDirect Internal 〕
                         │
        ┌────────────────┼────────────────┬────────────────┐
        ▼                ▼                ▼                ▼
   /logistics/le-wm  /logistics/le-shp  /sales/o2c       /manu/p2p
                                          │
                                          ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ LAYER 2: DOMAIN LAYER (Independent Ownership · iFlow: id01.sales...) │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Local Router    ──► Re-evaluates key: ORDERS05.ORDCHG.SAPPN1         │
│                     via local VM: i00.vm.o2c.routing.local...        │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Processing      ──► • Mapping & Enrichment  • Schema Validation      │
│                     • Custom JMS / DLQ       • Custom SLA & Alerting │
│                     • Target Adapter (HTTP, SOAP, RFC, OData)        │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

The Same Principle — Two Layers

The GDCR pattern in API Management uses the DDCR engine to resolve routing at runtime from a KVM. The proxy never changes. Key format: sales.order.create.sfdc → https://target/.... p50 = 0.34ms — sub-millisecond, zero regex, zero hardcode.

The same principle in CPI: one immutable iFlow, one Value Mapping that grows. Key format: ORDERS05.S4HCLNT100.100 → /pd/sales/o2c/order-inbound.

The Architecture — Two Immutable Layers

The solution uses two routing layers. Both follow the same pattern. Only the Value Mapping content differs.

SAP systems (any IDoc)
    ↓  [single SOAP endpoint]
Layer 1 — id01.shared.sap.idoc.pd.f.in.async    ← IMMUTABLE
    ↓  vm.shared.idoc.routing
ProcessDirect → Sales package · Finance package · Logistics package
    ↓
Layer 2 — id01.sales.pd.idoc.pd.f.in.async      ← IMMUTABLE
    ↓  vm.sales.o2c.idoc.routing
ProcessDirect → specific processing iFlow

The same Groovy script (sc.shared.idoc.channel-builder) runs at both layers. Zero code changes when a new IDoc type or new sender system is added.

The main IDOC VM - vm.shared.idoc.routing

The VM local in the Sales O2C - CPI Package - id00.o2c.vm.routing.idoc.metadata

The Composite Key — Three IDoc Header Fields

The routing decision is built from three fields of the IDoc Control Record (EDI_DC40), extracted by the upstream Content Modifier via native CPI XPath:

IDOC_TYP  ← //*[local-name()='IDOCTYP']/text()    e.g. ORDERS05
SNDSYS    ← //*[local-name()='SNDPRN']/text()      e.g. S4HCLNT100
MANDT ← //*[local-name()='MANDT']/text()      e.g. 100

The Groovy assembles the composite key:

def vmKey = "${idocTypRaw}.${sndSysRaw}".${sndSysRaw}"
// "ORDERS05.S4HCLNT100.100" → /pd/sales/o2c/order-inbound

One key. One lookup. One result. The SNDPRN identifies the sender division — S4HCLNT100 is acme, ERP1402 is acme eu — so the same IDoc type routes differently per division. No extra fields needed.

Value Mapping Configuration

Artifact: vm.shared.idoc.routing

Agency (source) Identifier (source) Source value → Target value

sap.idocidoc.control.typeORDERS05.S4HCLNT100.100→/pd/sales/o2c/order-inbound
sap.idocidoc.control.typeINVOIC02.S4HCLNT100.001→/pd/finance/r2r/invoice-inbound
sap.idocidoc.control.typeORDERS05.ERP1402.874→/pd/sales/o2c/order-inbound
sap.idocidoc.control.typeORDERS05.*→/pd/shared/idoc/deadletter

Agency semantics: sap.idoc — SAP owns the IDoc type code list. enterprise.integration — your company owns the routing target.

⚠️Source values are case-sensitive and must match the IDoc Control Record exactly — uppercase for IDOCTYP (ORDERS05, not orders05).

The composition of the key should also be adapt in your company, this is just sample.

Link for github - Download SAP CPI Packages, test POC in 10 min - git

The Groovy Script - Appendix A

ProcessDirect receiver address: ${property.TARGET_CHANNEL}

Exception Handling — Pluggable by Design

The pattern defines where exceptions go. What happens there is a company decision based on IDoc SLA.

When IS_MAPPED = false → TARGET_CHANNEL = 'unknown' → ProcessDirect routes to the dead-letter listener.

Option Implementation

A — MinimalData Store + manual replay via CPI Operations
B — AlertSend Email / Teams / Slack with VM_KEY and IDoc type
C — RetryJMS Queue with exponential backoff
D — FullData Store + Alert + JMS (enterprise grade)

⚠️The dead-letter listener must exist as a deployed iFlow before activating the FALLBACK. Start with Option A.

Before and After

  Traditional This pattern

Endpoint1 per IDoc type per system1 for everything
New IDoc typeNew iFlow + port + deployAdd 1 VM row
New SAP systemNew destination + portAdd VM rows
Routing logicHardcoded in iFlowValue Mapping only
TraceabilityPer-iFlow MPLVM_KEY in every MPL log

Resources

  • 📄ODCP v3.0: DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18876593
  • 📄SDIA v3.0: DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18877635
  • 📄GDCR + DDCR: DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18864833
  • 📸site: www.domain-intent.com

Conclusion

The iFlow never changes. The Value Mapping grows.
The proxy never changes. The KVM grows.

Same principle. Layer 1 (GDCR) and Layer 3 (ODCP) of the same architecture — SDIA.

When your landscape adds a new IDoc type, a new SAP system, or a new business division: add rows to a table. No developer. No deployment. No SAP port reconfiguration.

The iFlow is the contract. The Value Mapping is the configuration. They must never be confused.

References:

Optimized IDoc Routing in SAP CI with Value Mapping and Groovy-Based Receiver Determination

IDoc Dispatching - Use case for the ProcessDirect Adapter

SAP CPI Dynamic Router with External Rule Set using Camel Simple Expressions

Ricardo " Viana "
Independent Solo Researcher 
Creator of DEIP · SDIA · GDCR · DDCR · ODCP · EDCP · DDCP
ORCID: 0009-0009-9549-5862 
SDIA Ecosystem DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.19004802 · CC BY 4.0

Appendix - A

Groovy Script - Inside the Spoiler:

Spoiler
// =============================================================================
//  script collection : sc.shared.idoc.channel-builder
//  author            : Ricardo Viana — SAP BTP Integration Suite Expert
//  version           : 1.0.0
//  date              : 2026-05-07
// -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
//  functions exposed (call via iflow groovy step):
//    processData(message)        — main router: builds key, calls vm, sets props
//    validateRouting(message)    — guard before processdirect: throws if unmapped
//    buildErrorPayload(message)  — replaces body with json error on failure
// =============================================================================

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import com.sap.it.api.ITApiFactory
import com.sap.it.api.mapping.ValueMappingApi


// =============================================================================
//  function : processData
//  called by: groovy step in the global idoc router iflow
//  purpose  : reads exchange properties set by content modifier, builds the
//             composite key, performs vm lookup cascade, sets target_channel
// =============================================================================
def Message processData(Message message) {

    // ── vm coordinates — only config point in this script ────────────────────
    def vm_agency_src='sap.idoc'
    def vm_id_src='idoc.control.type'
    def vm_agency_tgt = 'enterprise.integration'
    def vm_id_tgt     = 'pd.routing.channel'
    def fallback      = 'unknown'

    def props = message.getProperties()

    // ── read raw — preserve case for vm lookup (case-sensitive) ──────────────
    def idoc_typ_raw = (props['idoc_typ'] as String)?.trim() ?: ''
    def msg_typ_raw  = (props['msg_typ']  as String)?.trim() ?: ''
    def snd_por_raw  = (props['snd_por']  as String)?.trim() ?: ''

    // ── normalise to lowercase for all downstream properties ──────────────────
    props.put('idoc_typ',  idoc_typ_raw.toLowerCase())
    props.put('idoc_extn', ((props['idoc_extn'] as String)?.trim() ?: '').toLowerCase())
    props.put('msg_typ',   msg_typ_raw.toLowerCase())
    props.put('snd_por',   snd_por_raw.toLowerCase())

    // ── composite key: idoctyp.mestyp.mescod.sndpor ──────────────────────────
    def vm_key         = "${idoc_typ_raw}.${msg_typ_raw}.${snd_por_raw}"
    def target_channel = fallback
    def vm_error       = ''

    try {
        def vm_api = ITApiFactory.getApi(ValueMappingApi.class, null)

        // lookup 1 — full key: idoctyp.mestyp.mescod.sndpor
        def mapped = vm_api.getMappedValue(
            vm_agency_src, vm_id_src, vm_key,
            vm_agency_tgt, vm_id_tgt
        )

        // lookup 2 — no mescod: idoctyp.mestyp.*.sndpor
        if (!mapped?.trim()) {
            mapped = vm_api.getMappedValue(
                vm_agency_src, vm_id_src,
                "${idoc_typ_raw}.${msg_typ_raw}.*.${snd_por_raw}",
                vm_agency_tgt, vm_id_tgt
            )
        }

        // lookup 3 — wildcard catch-all: idoctyp.*.*.*
        if (!mapped?.trim()) {
            mapped = vm_api.getMappedValue(
                vm_agency_src, vm_id_src, "${idoc_typ_raw}.*.*.*",
                vm_agency_tgt, vm_id_tgt
            )
        }

        if (mapped?.trim()) target_channel = mapped.trim()

    } catch (Exception e) {
        vm_error = e.message ?: 'unknown error'
    }

    // ── set output properties ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
    props.put('target_channel', target_channel)
    props.put('is_mapped',      (target_channel != fallback).toString())
    props.put('vm_key',         vm_key)
    props.put('vm_error',       vm_error)

    return message
}


// =============================================================================
//  function : validateRouting
//  called by: groovy step placed immediately BEFORE the processdirect step
//  purpose  : throws a meaningful exception when target_channel is unresolved,
//             replacing the generic processdirect null address error with a
//             clear, actionable message visible in the mpl
// =============================================================================
def Message validateRouting(Message message) {

    def props          = message.getProperties()
    def target_channel = (props['target_channel'] as String)?.trim() ?: ''
    def vm_key         = (props['vm_key']         as String)?.trim() ?: 'n/a'
    def vm_error       = (props['vm_error']        as String)?.trim() ?: ''
    def is_mapped      = (props['is_mapped']       as String)?.trim() ?: 'false'

    if (!target_channel || target_channel == 'unknown' || is_mapped == 'false') {
        throw new RuntimeException(
            """\
idoc routing failed — composite key not found in value mapping.

  composite key   : ${vm_key}
  target_channel  : ${target_channel ?: 'null'}
  vm artifact     : acme.core.domain.idoc.routing
  vm error        : ${vm_error ?: 'none'}
  action required : register key '${vm_key}' in the value mapping

check the value mapping artifact and add the missing entry.\
"""
        )
    }

    return message
}


// =============================================================================
//  function : buildErrorPayload
//  called by: groovy step inside the exception subprocess
//  purpose  : replaces the message body with a structured json error payload
//             avoids memory overhead of mpl attachments (sap note 3418765)
//             content-type is set to application/json for downstream consumers
// =============================================================================
def Message buildErrorPayload(Message message) {

    def props          = message.getProperties()
    def target_channel = (props['target_channel'] as String)?.trim() ?: 'unknown'
    def vm_key         = (props['vm_key']         as String)?.trim() ?: 'n/a'
    def vm_error       = (props['vm_error']        as String)?.trim() ?: 'none'
    def idoc_typ       = (props['idoc_typ']        as String)?.trim() ?: ''
    def msg_typ        = (props['msg_typ']         as String)?.trim() ?: ''
    def snd_por        = (props['snd_por']         as String)?.trim() ?: ''

    def error_map = [
        header: [
            script    : 'sc.shared.idoc.channel-builder v3.0.0',
            author    : 'Ricardo Viana - SAP BTP Integration Expert',
            timestamp : new Date().format("yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss"),
            severity  : 'error'
        ],
        input: [
            idoc_typ : idoc_typ,
            msg_typ  : msg_typ,
            snd_por  : snd_por
        ],
        routing: [
            composite_key  : vm_key,
            target_channel : target_channel,
            is_mapped      : false,
            vm_artifact    : 'acme.core.domain.idoc.routing',
            vm_error       : vm_error
        ],
        action_required: "register key '${vm_key}' in value mapping artifact 'acme.core.domain.idoc.routing'"
    ]

    message.setBody(
        groovy.json.JsonOutput.prettyPrint(
            groovy.json.JsonOutput.toJson(error_map)
        )
    )
    message.setHeader('Content-Type', 'application/json')

    return message
}

entry.title: Automate log level commands - SAP CPI
entry.content:
Introduction:

The interface is designed to run manually, timer-based, and through external systems.

API resource we used: /Operations/com.sap.it.op.tmn.commands.dashboard.webui.IntegrationComponentSetMplLogLevelCommand

Overview:

Managing log levels for SAP Cloud Integration artifacts becomes challenging when:

  • Multiple artifacts need log level changes simultaneously
  • Manual operations through "Manage Integration Content" are time-consuming
  • Troubleshooting scenarios require quick log level adjustments across related flows
  • Environment migrations need consistent log level configurations
  • Incident response demands rapid enabling of DEBUG/TRACE for multiple components

Limitations of manual process:

  • Navigate to each integration flow individually
  • Click through UI for each artifact
  • Risk of missing artifacts
  • No audit trail of changes
  • Time-consuming for 10+ artifacts

Common Scenarios when this can be helpful:

  1. Incident Response: Enable DEBUG/TRACE for all flows in a specific integration package
  2. Environment Migration: Apply consistent log levels across ENT/DEV/PROD
  3. Post-Deployment: Reset all flows to INFO after troubleshooting
  4. Scheduled Maintenance: Temporarily enable verbose logging during testing windows
  5. Package-Level Management: Set log levels for all flows in a business domain (e.g., all "Sales Order_*" flows)

In short: Use the API programmatically to perform bulk operations efficiently.

Log levels we majorly use:

Level

Description

When to Use

Performance Impact

NONE

No logging

Production (when monitoring not needed)

Minimal

INFO

Basic execution info

Standard production monitoring

Low

DEBUG

Detailed execution steps

Troubleshooting, development

Medium

TRACE

Full message payload & headers

Deep debugging, payload inspection

High

Integration flow design:

 

  • To run the interface both from cpi and external call (e.g. postman), we have added two main integration processes.
  • As we are passing a simple and formal payload hence converter is used in the first place after which the process call executes the local integration process.
  • In the local integration process, we are splitting the I flow ids if passed more than 1 in input.
  • After getting the I flow id, the same can be used to form the required payload which goes to the final local integration process through the process call.
  • We have configured 3 different receivers to differentiate the tenants i.e. ENT/DEV/PROD.
  • All the dynamic values are externalized nd configured in the runtime without explicitly modifying the interface.

Configuration example of http protocol.

  • We have created OAUTH credential using the token URL, Api key, Api secret from the specific tenant’s service key on BTP and the credential for all three environments i.e. ENT, DEV and PROD.
  • As the credentials are deployed in the security material on ENT tenant itself hence interface transport to the above environment is not needed.

Request example:

Header value to be passed for selecting the tenant:

Body value to be passed:

<IFLOWID>,<LOGLEVEL>

Response example:

logLevel,traceActive,traceEnabled,traceExpiresAt,iflowID

TRACE,true,true,Mon Apr 06 08:03:40 UTC 2026,BRAVO_Delivery_FSAP

ERROR,NA,true,NA,MDGPLM_MasterInspectionCharacteristics_GSAP

The response is in csv and to make it more readable we can open it in excel and the final response will look like this:

logLevel

traceActive

traceEnabled

traceExpiresAt

iflowID

TRACE

true

TRUE

Mon Apr 06 08:01:39 UTC 2026

BRAVO_Delivery_FSAP

ERROR

NA

TRUE

NA

MDGPLM_MasterInspectionCharacteristics_GSAP

Conclusion: 

The manual efforts can be reduced if it's used properly and is helpful majorly during the monitoring and troubleshooting where many of the interfaces in the same package can be put on an specific log level without explicitly touching the integration system again and again.

Important: Higher log levels (DEBUG, TRACE) generate more data and can impact system performance and storage. Use them temporarily for troubleshooting and revert to INFO or NONE for production.

Hope it helps someone someday, happy integrating 😊.

entry.title: Batch File Pickup and Dynamic ZIP Creation in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Picking Multiple Files and Creating ZIP Files in SAP Cloud Integration — A Complete Guide

Introduction  

While developing integrations in SAP Cloud Integration, file handling is one of the most common tasks encountered, and in modern integration scenarios, organizations deal with a large number of files arriving on the SFTP server that must be processed and sent to another third-party system. So, dealing with a large number of files at once can impact the system. To address this, files can be processed in batches so that additional operations can be performed and the interface runs smoothly.

This blog explains how batch file processing and ZIP file creation can be implemented in SAP Integration Suite to handle large volume of files efficiently.

Requirements:  

  • Pick 10 files from the source SFTP server
  • Combine the files into a ZIP archive and post it to another SFTP server

Condition: When the ZIP file is extracted, the original file names must remain unchanged  

This approach is especially useful for controlled batch processing, scheduled integrations, and cases that require dynamic file compression.  

 

Prerequisites — Server and Folder Setup  

Before getting started with the integration flow, ensure that the dedicated SFTP server is configured and the directories are created as shown below. 

Create the following directories on the server:  

  • inbound — Source directory where incoming files are placed   
  • tempstore — Temporary directory used to store appended batch data   
  • outbound — Target directory where final ZIP files are delivered

 

Step-by-Step Implementation  

Step 1 — Start by configuring the SFTP sender adapter to control the number of files processed during each run.  

Key Configuration  

Max Messages Per Poll = 10  (in this scenario) 

This ensures that only 10 files are picked in a single batch.  

 

 

Important Tip  

Using a scheduler with a controlled time interval is recommended.

The batch condition logic might not function as intended if the polling interval is set too frequently (for instance, every 10 seconds). Files may end up being processed individually rather than collectively.

Longer scheduling intervals guarantee that all necessary files are available before processing starts. 

In this scenario, 10 files are processed per batch. If more than 10 files are involved, a recurring condition (for example, “Every 5 min”) or even the Advanced Scheduler can be used to ensure that files are grouped and processed in consistent batches.

 

Step 2 — Store File Metadata Using Content Modifier  

After picking the files, the next step is to preserve both the filename and file content. This is necessary because once files are aggregated, their original metadata may be lost.  

Payload Structure:  

<payload>  
    <filename>${header.CamelFileName}</filename>  
    <body>  
        ${in.body}  
    </body>  
</payload>

This ensures:  

  • Filename is preserved  
  • File content is preserved  
  • Data can be safely aggregated and split later

 

Step 3 — Append All Files into a Single Storage File  

The Send step is used to write all payloads into a single file on a temporary SFTP location.  

This step uses Append Mode.  

Key Configuration  

 Append Mode = Enabled  

 

 

Step 4 — Detect Batch Completion Using Router  

A Router is used to determine whether all files in the batch have been processed.  

The router checks the following condition:  

${property.CamelBatchComplete} = 'true'

What This Means  

  • The property becomes true only after all files in the batch are processed  
  • Processing continues only when the batch is complete  
  • If not complete, the flow ends 

 

 

Step 5 — Read the Combined Data Using Poll Enrich  

Once the batch is complete, the system must retrieve the aggregated file.  

This is done using a Poll Enrich step.  

 

Note

"Before Poll Enrich, the message contains the last processed file, after using the Replace aggregation algorithm, the message is replaced with the combined batch file retrieved from temporary storage."  

The SFTP adapter should be configured accordingly. 

 

 

Step 6 — Add a Root Tag to the Combined Payload  

After retrieving the combined data, a Content Modifier is used to wrap the entire payload inside a root element.  

Payload Structure  

<data>  
${in.body}  
</data> 

Why is this required? 

  • To process the XML a single root element is required and without this, it may be difficult for the splitter to perform the split operation as the structure becomes invalid. 

 

Step 7 — Split the Combined Data into Individual Payloads  

A General Splitter is used to split the entire payload into individual messages. 

 

Step 8 — Extract Filename and Body Using Content Modifier  

With the help of this Content Modifier step, the file name and the data can be extracted from each payload (individual split message). 

Properties Created  

FileName  
DataBody  

Message Body Configuration  

${property.DataBody}  

This camel expression is used to call the file content into the message body of content modifier. 

 

 

Step 9 — Create ZIP Files Using Gather Step  

The Gather step is used to create ZIP file dynamically.  

Key Configuration  

  • Incoming Format = Any  
  • Aggregation Algorithm = ZIP  
  • File Name = ${property.FileName}  

Purpose  

  • Dynamically compress the files 
  • Original file names are preserved 

 

Step 10 — Send ZIP Files to Target SFTP Server  

This is the final step where the created ZIP file is sent to the receiver system. 

 

Scheduling Consideration (Critical) {Refer Step 1}  

The scheduling of the integration flow must be time-specific instead of running it continuously. 

Recommendation~ 

Set the scheduler in such as:  

  • At a fixed time on daily basis
  • Hourly basis 
  • At a particular event  

Why is this required? 

If the integration flow runs too frequently:

  • Files may be processed individually 
  • Files may not be picked in proper batches 
  • Aggregation logic may fail 

Scheduling ensures batch processing is controlled and efficient. 

Output / Result  

After deploying the integration flow, the system successfully processes files in controlled batches and generates a compressed output file.  

Observed Behavior  

  • 10 files are picked in a batch from the inbound folder 
  • A ZIP file is created using the Gather palette element  
  • ZIP filename: Batch20260425120549.zip  
  • Zip file is posted to receiver system 
  • When ZIP file is unzipped original file names are preserved

 

 Conclusion  

This blog post covered how to pick multiple files from SFTP using batch processing, combine them into a zip file using gather and post to another SFTP server, preserving file content and file names. 

I'd appreciate any insights, improvements or feedback on this approach. 

If you have questions about the configuration steps or want to share your experiences, please feel free to leave a comment below. 

Happy Integrating!!!

entry.title: Automating MPL and Payload Retrieval in SAP Integration Suite for Enhanced Monitoring
entry.content:
Why we build this solution,

The primary objective is to design a centralized and automated Monitoring solution capable of dynamically retrieving MPL Logs, extracting payload attachment and provide a reusable framework for alerting through Mails or other channel for Integration support activities.

If there is a specific requirement to identify the failed interfaces with Status Message, its Payloads with the exact error information across the Multiple packages within in the required Date and Time range. That required navigation through several monitoring. Now we can achieve this without going to the Monitoring page.

Introduction

Monitoring and troubleshooting integration flows in SAP Integration Suite can become complex when dealing with multiple packages and interfaces. Accessing Message processing Logs (MPL) and their Payload attachments is essential for debugging, Auditing, and Reporting.

This Blog demonstrates a fully Automated Approach to:

  • Fetch the MPL logs dynamically
  • Filter logs based on Time range and integration flow Name 
  • Retrieve payload attachments
  • Process and store Payload data

Step 1: Create OAuth2 client Credentials

Navigate to Monitor→ Integration and APIs→ Manage Security→ Security Material 

create a new credential of type OAuth 2 client credentials
Maintain:

  • Client Id
  • Client secret
  • Token Service URL

OAuth2 client credentials to securely connect to the SAP APIs.

Step 2: Implementation in Sap Integration Suite

Design an Integration Flow by using a Timer Trigger and then configure all the necessary content Modifier, this is where we need to set the control filtering Parameters for MPL Logs. 

Step 3: configure Http Receiver (MPL ODATA  API)

Configure HTTP Adapter:

  • Method:  GET
  • URL:
  • https://<tenant>/api/V1/MessageProcessingLogs 
  • Headers: Accept: application/Json

Step 4: Extract Message GUID

Parse the MPL response which we get from the O DATA API Call and extract the Message GUID (MPL_ID) for the further processing.

In the similar way we can configure the HTTP Adapter to call ODATA APIs to retrieve the attachments and its corresponding value. 

During the Implementation we were also able to extract the Failed Interfaces error Information for specific MPL ID and send the error logs for Monitoring.  Can be used for Error handling in the later stage of development.

 

Challenges faced during Implementation

One of the key challenges during the implementation was to handle the dynamic retrieval of payload attachment using SAP Integration OData APIs. Since the attachments, Metadata and the Payloads are retrieved through multiple APIs calls, designing the flow dynamically without hardcoded package name required careful handling of the MPL properties.

Another challenge we came across was managing the Namespace handling and the Splitter configuration while processing the XML response from APIs. Proper XPath handling and dynamically routing logic were essential to ensure the reliable payload extraction. And throughout the Designing and Execution of this particular Integration flow I have gained a deeper Learning and insight into SAP Integration suite Monitoring APIs and Dynamic I flow filtering.

 

Conclusion:

This Approach demonstrate how SAP Integration Suite Monitoring capabilities can be significantly Enhanced using automated MPL and payload retrieving mechanism to build a Robust monitoring solution in SAP Integration Suite, this can be further Enhanced to 

  • Integrate with Monitoring Dashboards
  • Automated Alert Mechanism detection
  • Dynamically retrieves MPL logs
  • Fetches payload Attachments
  • Supports audit and Analytics

This approach significantly improves visibility, automation, reduces downtime, and enhance operational efficiency in enterprise integrations.

entry.title: Implementing AVRO Serialization and Schema Registry with Kafka Receiver Adapter in Cloud Integration
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Cloud Integration (CI) provides a standard Kafka receiver adapter that allows integration flows to publish messages to Kafka topics. However, the adapter currently does not support AVRO serialization or Schema Registry integration natively. This limitation is already acknowledged by SAP and is listed as a long-term improvement item on the SAP Influence portal.

In one of our integration scenarios, we had a requirement to publish messages in Confluent AVRO wire format, with schemas managed centrally in a Schema Registry. Since the standard adapter does not yet support this, we implemented a custom solution using Groovy scripting and external libraries.

This blog post focuses on:

  • Working with Schema Registry
  • Handling AVRO serialization
  • Preparing messages for the Kafka receiver adapter

ℹ️ Basic Kafka adapter configuration and CI fundamentals are not covered here, as they are already well documented in SAP Help and SAP Integration Community.

 

Schema Registry

Kafka treats messages as raw byte arrays and does not enforce any structure on the data. This can easily lead to incompatibilities between producers and consumers.

A Schema Registry solves this problem by:

  • Acting as a central repository for message schemas
  • Assigning a unique schema ID to each schema version
  • Ensures both producers and consumers agree on the message structure

For a Kafka producer using Confluent AVRO format, the message must contain:

  • A schema ID
  • The payload serialized according to the corresponding AVRO schema

Therefore, before publishing a message to Kafka, the producer must know:

  1. The schema ID
  2. The schema definition (schema value)

 

Approaches to Retrieve Schema Information

First step is to get the Schema details for the AVRO conversion. We explored two possible options to retrieve the schema ID and schema value inside Cloud Integration capability of SAP Integration Suite.

Option 1: Calling the Schema Registry API

In this approach, the integration flow makes a HTTP call to the Schema Registry REST API to fetch the latest schema ID and schema value dynamically.

HTTP call to Schema Registry

Steps involved
  • Call the Schema Registry endpoint
  • Extract the schema ID and schema value from the response using a Groovy script
  • Store these values in Exchange Properties for later use
Pros
  • Always retrieves the latest schema
  • No manual updates required
Cons
  • Additional HTTP call for every message
  • Slight performance impact and extra cost

This approach is ideal when schemas change frequently or must always stay in sync.

 

Option 2: Storing Schema in Partner Directory (Chosen Approach)

The second approach is to store the schema value statically in the Partner Directory and retrieve it at runtime using a Groovy script. The Schema ID is externalized as a configurable parameter. We opted for this approach because our schemas do not change often.

Schema stored in Partner Directory

Steps involved
  • Store the schema value as a JSON file as Binary Parameters in Partner Directory
  • Extract the schema value in the integration flow using a Groovy script
  • Set the schema id as an externalized Exchange Property in the integration flow
Pros
  • It avoids repeated outbound HTTP calls
  • Improves performance and reduces runtime overhead
Cons
  • Whenever the schema changes in the Schema Registry, the schema value and schema ID in CI must be updated manually
    
//***Step 1 : Get the schema value from Partner Directory***//

    // Instance of Partner Directory Service
    def service = ITApiFactory.getApi(PartnerDirectoryService.class, null); 
    if (service == null){
        throw new IllegalStateException("Partner Directory Service not found");
    }
    
    // get the schema value from the Partner Directory
    def schemaBinary = service.getParameter(streamName, "SchemaRegistry" , BinaryData.class);
    
    // set the schema value to a property if it exists
    if (schemaBinary != null){
        byte[] schemaBytes = schemaBinary.getData()
        schemaValue = new String(schemaBytes, StandardCharsets.UTF_8)
    } else {
        throw new IllegalStateException("Schema not found for stream $streamName in Partner Directory");   
    }

 

Payload Conversion to AVRO Format

Once the schema ID and schema value are available, the next step is to convert the incoming message payload to AVRO format before sending it to Kafka.

Scenario Overview

  • Incoming payload: JSON
  • Target output: Confluent AVRO binary format

The conversion is done using a Groovy script with external libraries.

Inputs to the Groovy Script

The Groovy script requires:

  • Input JSON payload
  • AVRO schema definition
  • Schema ID

Output of the Groovy Script in Confluent Wire Format

The output binary produced by the script follows the Confluent serialization format:

[Magic Byte][Schema ID][Serialized Data]

  • Byte 0 – Magic byte (always 0 for AVRO)
  • Bytes 1–4 – 4‑byte schema ID
  • Remaining bytes – AVRO‑serialized record data

The Kafka receiver adapter publishes this binary data directly to the topic.

External Libraries Used

To achieve the conversion, the following libraries were added as external resources:

  • Apache Avro
  • Jackson (for JSON parsing)
Since this solution relies on external libraries and the AVRO conversion logic can be reused across multiple integration scenarios, it is recommended to implement it as a reusable Script Collection. This approach helps keep individual integration flows lightweight by avoiding repeated library uploads, and it also simplifies maintenance by centralizing the logic in one place.
//***Step 2 : Convert the JSON input to AVRO***//

    ByteArrayOutputStream output = null;
    try {
        def schema = new Schema.Parser().parse(schemaValue);

        // Convert JSON to Avro bytes
        AvroSchema jacksonAvroSchema = new AvroSchema(schema);
        AvroMapper avroMapper = new AvroMapper();
        output = new ByteArrayOutputStream();
        avroMapper.writer(jacksonAvroSchema).writeValue(output, new ObjectMapper().readTree(payload));
        byte[] avroBytes = output.toByteArray();

        // Build Confluent format: [magic byte][schema ID][data]
        ByteBuffer buffer = ByteBuffer.allocate(1 + 4 + avroBytes.length)
        buffer.put((byte) 0)
        buffer.putInt(Integer.parseInt(schemaId.trim()))
        buffer.put(avroBytes)
        buffer.flip()

        // Set header and body
        message.setHeader("kafka.KEY", "")
        message.setBody(buffer.array());

    } catch (Exception e) {
        throw new RuntimeException("Failed to convert JSON to Avro format: " + e.getMessage(), e);
    } finally {
        // Close output stream to prevent resource leaks
        if (output != null) {
            try {
                output.close();
            } catch (Exception e) {
                messageLog.addCustomHeaderProperty("Warning", "ByteArrayOutputStream cannot be closed. " + e.getMessage());
            }
        }
        // Close reader to prevent resource leaks
        if (payload != null) {
            try {
                payload.close();
            } catch (Exception e) {
                messageLog.addCustomHeaderProperty("Warning", "Input Reader cannot be closed. " + e.getMessage());
            }
        }
    }

 

Alternative Approach

It is also possible to implement the solution using only the Apache Avro libraries, without relying on Jackson. However, in our experience, this approach led to challenges when the AVRO schema contained optional fields defined as unions with null (for example, "type": ["null", "string"]).

If your schema does not include union fields, this approach is a better option.

    // Convert JSON to Avro object    
        def reader = new GenericDatumReader<Object>(schema)    
        Decoder decoder = DecoderFactory.get().jsonDecoder(schema, payload)    
        Object datum = reader.read(null, decoder)
       
    // Serialize Avro object to binary   
        def writer = new GenericDatumWriter<Object>(schema)    
        Encoder encoder = EncoderFactory.get().binaryEncoder(output, null)    
        writer.write(datum, encoder)    
        encoder.flush()    
        byte[] avroBytes = output.toByteArray()

 

Conclusion

Until SAP provides native support for AVRO and Schema Registry in the Kafka adapter, this custom approach offers a way to:

  • Work with Schema Registry
  • Serialize messages into Confluent AVRO format
  • Publish compatible messages to Kafka from SAP Cloud Integration 

Finally, I would like to thank Sunny Kapoor for his valuable insights, and support in shaping this blog post.

entry.title: SAP BTP - API Management with SDIA — GDCR & DDCR in Practice
entry.content:
"The proxy never changes. The KVM grows."

Introduction

This is not a product. There is no license fee, no consulting package, and no vendor behind it.

SDIA — Semantic Domain Integration Architecture — was created in three months, solo, without funding, driven by one frustration I kept seeing across SAP BTP projects: the same mistakes repeated at scale.

Dozens of proxies doing the same thing.
No deterministic naming convention.
Credentials mixed across domains.
A developer required for every new route.
A deployment cycle for every new endpoint.
Monitoring showing dozens of iFlows doing variations of the same architectural function.

I built SDIA because the SAP community deserved better than that.

SDIA lives in the Domain Layer. It is governance, not code. It is architecture, not a framework you install. Anyone can adopt it, adapt it, and evolve it — that is the point.

This blog demonstrates two SDIA patterns applied to SAP BTP API Management:

Pattern Role
GDCR — Gateway Domain-Centric RoutingImmutable semantic gateway facade
DDCR — Domain Driven-Centric RouterDeterministic runtime resolution engine

Both are running live in the demo environment shown in the video. Every proxy, every KVM entry, and every Newman call used in the demonstration is available on GitHub for download and testing.

The Problem This Solves

When teams migrate to SAP BTP Integration Suite, a predictable collapse often happens. What starts as a clean slate becomes:

  • one proxy per interface, vendor, or backend system;
  • no consistent naming across packages;
  • credentials shared across domains;
  • every new API route requiring code changes and redeployment;
  • monitoring dashboards filled with many artifacts doing variations of the same job.

This is what SDIA calls Endpoint Chaos and Package Sprawl.

It is not incompetence. It is the natural result of building without a governing principle.

The governing principle SDIA introduces is simple:

The domain is the primary key of every integration artifact.

From the API gateway to the integration layer to the data tier — one semantic key, applied consistently, reduces structural chaos.

This is what happens when enterprise integration is indexed by tools, vendors, projects, and protocols instead of domains.

This is not a tooling problem. It is an indexing problem.

The landscape is indexed by technology. SDIA re-indexes it by domain.

What SDIA Is

SDIA — Semantic Domain Integration Architecture — is a vendor-agnostic governance model. It defines how integration artifacts are named, how packages are structured, how credentials are segmented, and how routing decisions are made across the enterprise integration landscape.

For this blog, three layers matter:

Layer Pattern Governs
 
Layer 1GDCRSAP BTP API Management — semantic gateway facade
Layer 2DDCRSAP BTP API Management — deterministic runtime resolution
Layer 3ODCPSAP Cloud Integration — iFlow, package, credential, and Value Mapping governance

GDCR and DDCR operate inside SAP API Management. ODCP operates inside SAP Cloud Integration.

Together, they form an immutable routing pipeline where the deployed artifact does not change when a new route is added. The metadata grows.

The same principle was also applied to SAP Cloud Integration using ODCP and Value Mapping:

Companion implementation for Layer 3:

SAP BTP - CI - Immutable Routing Principle: in SAP CPI with Value Mapping metadata ODCP in practice
Released: 07/05/2026

 

The Architecture — Immutable Facade, Metadata Resolution

External HTTP/API Consumer
(any system consuming the semantic API facade)
        │
        ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                      SAP BTP API Management                          │
│                                                                      │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │ LAYER 1 — GDCR                                                 │  │
│  │ Gateway Domain-Centric Routing                                 │  │
│  │                                                                │  │
│  │ Responsibility:                                                │  │
│  │   • expose one immutable semantic gateway facade per domain    │  │
│  │   • validate the semantic URL contract                         │  │
│  │   • apply authentication / access policies                     │  │
│  │   • preserve domain intent                                     │  │
│  │                                                                │  │
│  │ Example Semantic URL:                                          │  │
│  │   /sales/orders/create/salesforce                              │  │
│  └───────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                  │                                   │
│                                  ▼                                   │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │ LAYER 2 — DDCR                                                 │  │
│  │ Domain Driven-Centric Router                                   │  │
│  │                                                                │  │
│  │ Responsibility:                                                │  │
│  │   • normalize the action                                       │  │
│  │   • build the deterministic routing key                        │  │
│  │   • resolve the backend target from KVM metadata               │  │
│  │   • fail fast when no governed route exists                    │  │
│  │   • set the resolved target.url                                │  │
│  └───────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                  │                                   │
└──────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────┘
                                   │
                                   ▼
                    ┌──────────────────────────────┐
                    │ Metadata Control Plane       │
                    │ kvm.idinterface              │
                    │                              │
                    │ route key → target profile   │
                    └───────────────┬──────────────┘
                                    │
        ┌───────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────┐
        ▼                           ▼                           ▼
┌───────────────────┐     ┌────────────────────┐      ┌────────────────────┐
│ SAP CPI iFlow     │     │ Registered HTTP    │      │ SOAP/CXF-backed    │
│ SOAP/HTTP         │     │ backend            │      │ integration target │
│ ODCP governed     │     │                    │      │ via CPI mediation  │
└───────────────────┘     └────────────────────┘      └────────────────────┘

GDCR is the entry point to the Domain Layer. It is the contract between the outside world and the integration landscape. It never hardcodes a backend target.

DDCR is the deterministic runtime resolver. It receives the semantic request, resolves the target through metadata, and binds the request to the registered backend profile.

The gateway facade remains stable. The metadata evolves.

The KVM — Where Runtime Variability Lives

The KVM — Key Value Map — is the metadata control plane used by DDCR behind the GDCR facade.

It is the only thing that grows when a new route is added.

Each domain package has its own kvm.idinterface. In this demo, keys are domain-local. That means the route key does not need to repeat the domain token because the metadata container itself is already domain-scoped.

Demo key format:

dcrp{entity}{actioncode}{target}idNN:{protocol}

Semantic meaning:

entity      = business object
actioncode  = normalized DDCR action code
target      = vendor/system identifier
idNN        = route identifier
protocol    = registered target profile, for example http or cxf

Example:

dcrporderscsalesforceid01:http

Means:

orders  → entity
c       → create
salesforce → target
id01    → route id
http    → target profile

If an enterprise chooses to use a shared KVM across domains, the key should include the domain or use a full deterministic key. For this demo, the KVM is domain-scoped, which keeps the key compact.

 

SAP APIM — 4 Domain Proxies:

Domain-scoped KVMs:

SALES — kvm.idinterface

Package: cpipackage-nx.sales.o2c.integrations

Key Target
dcrporderscsalesforceid01:httphttps://<apim>/sales/orders/create/salesforce
dcrpordersusalesforceemeaid02:httphttps://<apim>/sales/orders/update/salesforceemea
dcrpcustomerssshopifyid03:httphttps://<apim>/sales/customers/sync/shopify
dcrppaymentsnstripeid04:httphttps://<apim>/sales/payments/notify/stripe
dcrporderscmicrosoftid05:cxfhttps://<apim>/sales/orders/create/microsoft
dcrpdeliveriestfedexid06:httphttps://<apim>/sales/deliveries/transfer/fedex
dcrpcustomersss4hanaid07:cxfhttps://<apim>/sales/customers/sync/s4hana
dcrppaymentsns4hanaid08:cxfhttps://<apim>/sales/payments/notify/s4hana
dcrpinvoicescquickbooksid09:cxfhttps://<apim>/sales/invoices/create/quickbooks
dcrpinvoicescs4hanaid10:cxfhttps://<apim>/sales/invoices/create/s4hana
dcrpdeliveriests4hanaid11:cxfhttps://<apim>/sales/deliveries/transfer/s4hana
dcrpreturnscshopifyid12:httphttps://<apim>/sales/returns/create/shopify

FINANCE — kvm.idinterface

Package: cpipackage-nx.finance.r2r.integrations

Key Target
dcrpinvoicescquickbooksid01:cxfhttps://<apim>/finance/invoices/create/quickbooks
dcrpinvoicescs4hanaid02:cxfhttps://<apim>/finance/invoices/create/s4hana
dcrppaymentsnstripeid03:httphttps://<apim>/finance/payments/notify/stripe
dcrppaymentsns4hanaid04:httphttps://<apim>/finance/payments/notify/s4hana
dcrpaccountssxeroid05:httphttps://<apim>/finance/accounts/sync/xero
dcrpjournalscsapid06:cxfhttps://<apim>/finance/journals/create/sap
dcrpexpensesccoupaid07:httphttps://<apim>/finance/expenses/create/coupa
dcrpreceiptsuconcurid08:httphttps://<apim>/finance/receipts/update/concur
dcrpbudgetssworkdayid09:cxfhttps://<apim>/finance/budgets/sync/workday
dcrptaxescavalaraid10:httphttps://<apim>/finance/taxes/create/avalara

LOGISTICS — kvm.idinterface

Package: cpipackage-nx.logistics.le.integrations

Key Target
dcrpshipmentscfedexid01:httphttps://<apim>/logistics/shipments/create/fedex
dcrptrackingsuupsid02:httphttps://<apim>/logistics/trackings/update/ups
dcrpdeliveriescdhlid03:cxfhttps://<apim>/logistics/deliveries/create/dhl
dcrpshipmentsqfedexid04:httphttps://<apim>/logistics/shipments/query/fedex
dcrpcontainerssmaerskid05:httphttps://<apim>/logistics/containers/sync/maersk
dcrpwarehousesuwmsid06:cxfhttps://<apim>/logistics/warehouses/update/wms
dcrpfreightsccoyoteid07:httphttps://<apim>/logistics/freights/create/coyote
dcrproutessproject44id08:httphttps://<apim>/logistics/routes/sync/project44
dcrpmanifestsccustomsid09:cxfhttps://<apim>/logistics/manifests/create/customs
dcrpinventoryswmsid10:cxfhttps://<apim>/logistics/inventory/sync/wms

PROCUREMENT — kvm.idinterface

Package: cpipackage-nx.procurement.p2p.integrations

Key Target
dcrprequisitionscaribaid01:cxfhttps://<apim>/procurement/requisitions/create/ariba
dcrpposccoupaid02:httphttps://<apim>/procurement/pos/create/coupa
dcrprfqscaribaid03:cxfhttps://<apim>/procurement/rfqs/create/ariba
dcrpcontractssjaggaerid04:httphttps://<apim>/procurement/contracts/sync/jaggaer
dcrpinvoicesabaswareid05:cxfhttps://<apim>/procurement/invoices/approve/basware
dcrpsupplierssivaluaid06:httphttps://<apim>/procurement/suppliers/sync/ivalua
dcrpcatalogsutradeshiftid07:httphttps://<apim>/procurement/catalogs/update/tradeshift
dcrpgrnscwmsid08:cxfhttps://<apim>/procurement/grns/create/wms
dcrpbuyerssoracleid09:httphttps://<apim>/procurement/buyers/sync/oracle
dcrpsourcingsqaribaid10:cxfhttps://<apim>/procurement/sourcing/query/ariba

URL Structure — APIM to CPI

The URL structure follows a deterministic semantic pattern. Once the convention is understood, every endpoint becomes self-documenting.

External APIM proxy URL:

https://{tenant}.hana.ondemand.com/{domain}/{entity}/{action}/{target}

Example — Sales Order from Salesforce entering the GDCR facade:

POST https://<apim>/sales/orders/create/salesforce

The request expresses intent:

domain  = sales
entity  = orders
action  = create
target  = salesforce

DDCR normalizes the action, builds the deterministic route key, resolves the target profile from kvm.idinterface, and sets the backend target dynamically.

The consumer sees:

/sales/orders/create/salesforce

The backend topology stays hidden.

CPI iFlow Address — ODCP Layer

When the target is SAP Cloud Integration, the target iFlow follows ODCP naming:

id{nn}.{subdomain}.{vendor}.{resource}.{system}.{operation}.{direction}.{mode}

Examples:

id01.o2c.salesforce.order.sap.c.in.sync
id07.o2c.s4hana.customer.sap.s.in.sync
id03.r2r.stripe.payment.s4hana.n.in.sync
id09.p2p.ariba.rfq.s4hana.c.in.sync

The protocol suffix in the KVM — for example :http or :cxf — tells DDCR which registered target profile to select.

Payload transformation remains the responsibility of the target integration flow.

GDCR — Responsibility and Boundaries

GDCR is the immutable semantic gateway to the Domain Layer.

What GDCR does

  • exposes a stable semantic facade;
  • receives inbound calls through a domain-level gateway contract;
  • applies authentication and API policies;
  • validates the semantic URL shape;
  • preserves domain intent;
  • hands the request to DDCR for runtime resolution.

What GDCR does not do

  • it does not transform the payload;
  • it does not own business logic;
  • it does not hardcode backend URLs;
  • it does not change when a new backend target is onboarded.

This boundary is the core of the pattern.

GDCR is the facade. DDCR is the resolver. CPI is the execution layer.

DDCR — Responsibility and Boundaries

DDCR is the deterministic domain resolution engine.

What DDCR does

  • receives the semantic request from the GDCR facade;
  • normalizes the action;
  • builds the deterministic routing key;
  • reads the domain KVM metadata;
  • resolves the registered target profile;
  • sets the resolved target.url;
  • fails fast when no governed route exists;
  • optionally captures routing timing headers in benchmark/demo mode.

What DDCR does not do

  • it does not own business logic;
  • it does not validate business payload content;
  • it does not own retry policy;
  • it does not perform domain transformations;
  • it does not require code changes when a new target is registered.

Retry, mapping, enrichment, and business validation belong to the target integration flow in SAP Cloud Integration.

Before and After

Area Before SDIA After GDCR + DDCR

Proxies per domain1 per interface/vendor/backend1 per domain
New vendor onboardingNew proxy + deploymentAdd 1 KVM row
New operationNew route logic / proxy changeAdd 1 KVM row
Credential scopeMixed across domainsSegmented per domain
Naming conventionInconsistentDeterministic ODCP / SDIA naming
Routing logicHardcoded in proxyResolved from KVM
Developer required for new routeUsually yesNo routing-code developer
Redeployment required for new routeUsually yesNo proxy redeployment
Backend topology exposureVisible in URLHidden behind semantic facade

Validation — Newman Parallel Load Test

The architecture was validated using Newman with 4 parallel sessions and 2 iterations across all 42 endpoints — 336 total calls across SALES, FINANCE, LOGISTICS, and PROCUREMENT domains.

The test covered:

  • REST-style JSON payloads;
  • SOAP-style XML payloads;
  • HTTP target profiles;
  • CXF target profiles;
  • 4 domain metadata containers;
  • 42 KVM routes.

All 42 endpoints resolved correctly through KVM metadata.

Result:

42 endpoints
336 calls
0 routing failures
0 misrouted calls

The collection is available on GitHub and can be executed in any SAP BTP trial account.

Basic run:

newman run GDCR-DDCR-Phantom-v12.json -n 2

Parallel load simulation on Windows:

start "S1" cmd /k newman run GDCR-DDCR-Phantom-v12.json -n 10
start "S2" cmd /k newman run GDCR-DDCR-Phantom-v12.json -n 10
start "S3" cmd /k newman run GDCR-DDCR-Phantom-v12.json -n 10
start "S4" cmd /k newman run GDCR-DDCR-Phantom-v12.json -n 10

The Principle That Connects Everything

In SAP Cloud Integration, ODCP applies the same logic to iFlows and Value Mappings:

The iFlow routing never changes. The Value Mapping grows.

In SAP BTP API Management, GDCR and DDCR apply the same logic to proxies and KVMs:

The proxy never changes. The KVM grows.

Same principle. Different layer. One architecture: SDIA.

When the landscape adds a new vendor, a new operation, or a new SAP system, the change surface becomes metadata.

No proxy redeployment.
No routing-code change.
No topology exposure in the consumer contract.

The proxy is the contract.
The KVM is the metadata control plane.
They must never be confused.

Download and Try It

Everything demonstrated in this post is available on GitHub:

  • APIM proxy artifacts for GDCR + DDCR;
  • KVM templates for all 4 domains;
  • Newman collection with parallel load test script;
  • SAP CPI iFlow stubs for target execution;
  • runnable test examples.

GitHub:

https://github.com/rhviana/deip/tree/main/gdcr-proven/gdcr-drcp-sap-api

References

SDIA v3.0          — DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18877635 — CC BY 4.0
ODCP v3.0          — DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18876593 — CC BY 4.0
DDCR V3.0          — DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.18864833 — CC BY 4.0

Site:

www.domain-intent.com

Ricardo " Viana "
Independent Solo Researcher 
Creator of DEIP · SDIA · GDCR · DDCR · ODCP · EDCP · DDCP
ORCID: 0009-0009-9549-5862

entry.title: Portable Local SAML Assertion Generator for SuccessFactors APIs (No Installations required etc.)
entry.content:
INTRODUCTION

Here is another way to gerenerate a signed SAML 2.0 bearer assertion locally without any installation for testing SFAPIs.

What It Is

A portable Windows GUI. Unzip, double-click SAMLAssertionGen.bat, done. Python runtime is bundled so there is nothing to install. Language refinement and code development done with the help of AI.

Same assertion logic as the Java original. Same XML structure, same RSA-SHA256 signature, same www.successfactors.com issuer and audience. I didn't reinvent anything, just made it easier to run.

Fill in the fields. Click Generate. Copy to clipboard.

You may save your own SAMLAssertion.properties file so values load automatically or just fill up the GUI.

Setup

1. Unzip anywhere.
2. Edit SAMLAssertion.properties:
 
  • tokenUrl - SF OAuth token endpoint
  • clientId - SF API key
  • userId - SF API user
  • userName - fallback if userId is empty
  • privateKey — Base64-encoded PKCS#8 RSA key downloaded from SF - Manage OAuth2 Client Applications
  • expireInMinutes — default 10, keep it short
 
3. Run SAMLAssertionGen.bat.
4. Click Generate Assertion, then Copy to Clipboard.

 

Using the Assertion

POST {tokenUrl} Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

company_id={companyId}&client_id={clientId}&grant_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:saml2-bearer&assertion={your Base64 assertion}

In Postman: Body > raw > paste the form string above. The response returns a Bearer token. Add it as an Authorization header on any subsequent API call.

 

 

 

 

Technical Notes

XML mirrors the Java original Issuer and Audience set to www.successfactors.com, NotBefore backdated 10 minutes for clock skew, enveloped XML DSIG with RSA-SHA256. Libraries: lxml, signxml, cryptography — all bundled.

Security

  • Never commit your properties file. It contains your private key.
  • Treat the assertion like a password. It grants API access until expiry.
  • Keep expiration short. 10 minutes is the right default.

Download and extract the ZIP file:

https://github.com/wes-teros14/PortableSAMLAssertionGen

 

Conclusion

This utility simplifies the process of generating signed SAML assertions for SuccessFactors API testing by removing installation steps and packaging everything into a portable executable setup.

entry.title: Best Practices Pipeline for Cloud Integration: Naming Conventions
entry.content:
Co-authored by Slava Kuzyakin.

If you like to run your integration scenarios on the Pipeline for Cloud Integration, you need to create your scenario-specific integration flows as well as various entries in the Partner Directory. Furthermore, you have some flexibility in configuring and setting up the pipeline(s) and pipeline steps, i.e., the integration packages and generic integration flows that are provided on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub. Before you start working with the Pipeline, it's worthwhile to spend some time to define your own naming conventions which allow you to setup your integration scenarios and the pipelines in a common way.

This blog post is intended to help you defining your own naming conventions. They are not binding, they are rather meant as a recommendation. At the end, naming conventions pretty much depend on your own requirements and policies and hence can be applied in different ways. However, the guidelines provided here can be seen as a good starting point and, if necessary, can be extended or modified to your needs.

Partner Directory entries

In the Partner Directory, you have the option to create partner IDs for different purposes:

  • A Configuration Scenario defines how an integration scenario is configured and how a message behaves, e.g., whether or not an inbound conversion is needed, what xpath conditions should be carried out in a content based router pattern, how many retries should be done in case of a message failure before the message is sent to a dead letter queue, etc.
  • A System Alias represents your communication component, whether sender or receiver, within your pipeline. It is independent of your system landscape.
  • A Business Component is a communication component which name is independent of the system landscape, e.g., a communication component representing a file share.
  • A Business System is a communication component which name depends on the system landscape like for scenarios that are connected via XI or IDoc protocol where the system name on the DEV landscape differs from the system name on the PROD landscape. A business system is mapped to a system alias via an alternative partner in the Partner Directory entry of the system alias.
  • For IDocs, we have lately introduced the partner ID type representing a Party Alias. This can be used for partners of type not equal LS where we map the IDoc control record fields that describe the partner to a party alias.

Naming conventions:

  • Use a prefix for your configuration scenario names like CS_
  • We would treat system alias and business components the same, so that you have the option to define a business component as business system at a later point of time with no need to change the configuration scenario if you decide otherwise. So, here you can use the same prefix BC_
  • For the business systems, you have to stick to the names of your existing systems in your landscape, so here you follow your own naming conventions anyway. Otherwise, you may use the prefix BS_
  • For the party alias, you may use the prefix PRTY_

Scenario-specific integration flows

For the scenario-specific integration flows like for inbound processing, inbound conversion, outbound processing, various custom exits, etc., you can actually follow the same naming convention that we apply when generating those integration flows using the migration tool. If you use the migration tool, right now you need to stick to our naming convention anyway. Note, this will change soon, there are plans to make the proposed integration flow names in the migration tool editable.

In the migration tool, the following naming conventions apply in general:

  • Integration flow name: <scenario name>_PIP<pipeline step number>_<pipeline step description>[_<party name>][_<system name>][_IFIndex_<index number>]
  • ProcessDirect endpoint if applicable: /<scenario name>/pip<pipeline step number>[/<party name>][/<system name>][/IFIndex<index number>]

The <scenario name> is the name of your configuration scenario as defined in the Partner Directory.

PIP stands for Process Integration Pipeline. You may omit it and just have the pipeline step number on its own.

The <pipeline step number> corresponds to the sequence of the pipeline steps that a message is processed within the pipeline although not always all pipeline steps are carried out.

The <pipeline step description> shortly describes the pipeline step. You may omit it if you like to shorten the name.

Depending on the pipeline step, you need to add a system and optionally a party if you communicate to a business partner. The <system name> can be either a sender system or a receiver system depending on the context.

The <index number> of the receiver interface applies to the scenario-specific outbound processing flow only. if you have multiple interfaces for the same receiver, the index number is incremented, see below.

Scenario-specific inbound processing

So, for the scenario-specific inbound processing flow, the following applies:

  • Integration flow name: <scenario name>_PIP01_InboundProcessing

In case of a sender wildcard scenario where multiple sender systems reuse the same configuration scenario, the following applies:

  • Integration flow name: <scenario name>_PIP01_InboundProcessing[_<party name>]_<system name>

Example: CS_Scenario_A01_PIP01_InboundProcessing_BC_System_ABC (for a system without party)

Scenario-specific inbound conversion

For the scenario-specific inbound conversion flow, the following applies:

  • Integration flow name: <scenario name>_PIP03_InboundConversion
  • ProcessDirect endpoint: /<scenario name>/pip03

In case of a sender wildcard scenario, each sender may have a different inbound conversion, so we need an own integration flow for each sender system for which an inbound conversion should be carried out:

  • Integration flow name: <scenario name>_PIP03_InboundConversion[_<party name>]_<system name>
  • ProcessDirect endpoint: /<scenario name>/pip03[/<party name>]/<system name>

Example: CS_Scenario_A01_PIP03_InboundConversion_BC_System_ABC with endpoint /CS_Scenario_A01/pip03/BC_System_ABC

Scenario-specific outbound processing

For each receiver and corresponding receiver interface, an own scenario-specific outbound processing flow is generated. If a receiver has multiple receiver interfaces, an interface index number is incremented. We do not use the receiver interface name here because it may not be ambiguous, you may have xpath conditions for determining the interfaces with the same receiver interface name but different mappings for instance.

So, here the following applies:

  • Integration flow name: <scenario name>_PIP07_OutboundProcessing[_<party name>]_<system name>_IFIndex_<index number>
  • ProcessDirect endpoint: /<scenario name>/pip07[/<party name>]/<system name>/IFIndex<index number>

Example: CS_Scenario_A01_PIP07_OutboundConversion_BC_System_XYZ_IFIndex_1 with endpoint /CS_Scenario_A01/pip07/BC_System_XYZ/IFIndex1

Custom receiver determination

In case of extended receiver determination, where the receiver is determined via a message mapping, the message mapping can be reused and a custom receiver determination flow is generated instead of an XSLT.

So, here the following applies:

  • Integration flow name: <scenario name>_PIP04_ReceiverDetermination
  • ProcessDirect endpoint: /<scenario name>/pip04/customXRD

So, actually here the endpoint does not strictly follow the overall rule because a suffix is added indicating that the end point relates to a custom receiver determination.

Creating integration flows from templates

When you manually create the scenario-specific flows, you can apply the same or similar naming conventions like we do in the migration tool. You do not need to create the integration flows from scratch, we actually strongly recommend to copy the provided template flow from the Cloud Integration Pipeline - Templates package. This saves you a lot of effort and it ensures that the allowed header list and the exception subprocess is already properly defined.

Note: Each template comes with a description briefly describing what the template is good for. If you copy the template to your own integration flow, the description of the template is kept. So, you may need to change the description in the copied integration flow's metadata.

Setting up your pipeline(s)

A couple of versions ago, we have split up the provided integration flows in four packages to be more flexible in setting up your pipeline, see New integration packages for the Pipeline for Cloud Integration. We distinguish the following use cases:

  • You like to setup multiple pipelines to distribute your messages across multiple pipelines or to have dedicated pipelines in a shared tenant environment
  • You like to use a dedicated JMS queue for a particular receiver system
  • For sending IDoc or XI messages to Cloud Integration, you may setup dedicated generic IDoc/XI inbound processing flows for each sender system to be able for instance to define a sender-specific user role or to stop incoming messages for a specific sender system
  • For sending IDoc or XI messages from Cloud Integration to the backend system, you may setup dedicated generic IDoc/XI outbound processing flows acting as reusable channels

To setup your pipeline supporting those uses cases, you have to copy the respective packages from the SAP Business Accelerator Hub (actually from the Discovery area in your tenant) to the Design area of your tenant. This ensures that the copied packages are not cut off from future updates, see Updates for SAP's Integration Packages. When you copy those provided packages, you are told that the package already exists and hence to avoid overwriting the existing packages you must add a suffix to the copied package. This suffix is then also added to the ID of the integration flows within the copied packages.

Additional pipeline

If you setup an additional pipeline, you need to copy the three packages Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Inbound, Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Pipelines and Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Outbound. Once you have copied the packages, you need to configure the pipeline prefix (default PIP) of the generic integration flows. For the async pipeline steps, the pipeline prefix determines the JMS queue names. For the sync pipeline, it determines the ProcessDirect endpoint of the sync pipeline step.

So, best practice is to use the same or at least a similar name for both the package suffix and the pipeline prefix. Although you need to be aware of the following restrictions and hence may have to adapt the names accordingly.

  • The package suffix supports the following characters: [A-Za-z0-9.]
  • The JMS queue supports the following characters: [A-Za-z0-9_]
  • In total, the JMS queue name must not exceed 80 characters

Examples:

  • Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Pipelines.PIPXL with pipeline prefix PIPXL
  • Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Pipelines.ORG.ABC with pipeline prefix ORG_ABC

Dedicated inbound/outbound channel

To setup a dedicated inbound channel, you need to copy the package Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Inbound, for a dedicated outbound channel it's the package Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Outbound.

As suffix of the copied package, you can use the business system alias. You may also add a keyword like BC indicating that this package relates to a business component. If you like to define the inbound/outbound channel for a specific pipeline other than the default pipeline, then you can also combine the channel naming with the pipeline naming as described above.

For the use case of a receiver-specific queues, in the Pipeline v2 Generic Step06 - Outbound Processing flow, you actually have the option to define both a pipeline JMS queue prefix and a pipeline JMS queue suffix. Prefix, the fixed queue number Q04 and the suffix will be concatenated building the JMS queue name. Here, the suffix would also be the business system alias taking into account the character restrictions as mentioned above.

Examples:

  • Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Inbound.BC.UVW100 for business component UVW100
  • Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Inbound.PIPXL.BC.XYZ200 for business component XYZ200 on pipeline PIPXL
  • Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Outbound.BC.ABC.105 with pipeline JMS queue suffix ABC_105 (queue name PIPQ04ABC_105)

Hope those guidelines help you as starting point to define your own naming conventions. If you have feedback, let us know, please leave a comment at the bottom of the blog post.

entry.title: SAP BTP - APIM - DDCR - Deterministic Routing Mechanism JavaScript Policy full explanation each step
entry.content:
How a Single JavaScript Policy Routes 39 Tested SAP Integration Paths Across 4 Domains Without Hardcoded Business Routes

Zero regex. Zero split. Zero hardcoded business routes. The KVM defines the route.

This post explains the GDCR Routing Engine — Phantom v12, a JavaScript policy used inside SAP BTP API Management to resolve semantic inbound URLs into deterministic SAP CPI target URLs.

This is not a universal plug-and-play router for every enterprise URL structure. It is a reference template: a compact, production-oriented base that can be adapted by companies according to their own URL depth, KVM structure, naming convention, routing granularity, and performance requirements.

The important distinction is this:

Phantom v12 is stable as a reference implementation, but it is not 100% automatically adaptable to every company landscape without adjustment.

If a company changes the URL grammar, KVM key format, route metadata model, adapter rules, or output headers, the script must be adapted. That is normal. The value of Phantom v12 is not that it magically fits every architecture. The value is that it gives a clear, deterministic, metadata-driven routing template that avoids hardcoded business endpoints inside the proxy.

 

1. Where This Script Lives in SDIA

Before opening the code, it is important to understand where this JavaScript policy sits.

SDIA — Semantic Domain Integration Architecture organizes the integration landscape around domain-owned artifacts and metadata-driven routing. Inside SAP BTP API Management, two runtime patterns are relevant:

External Client
      │
      ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ LAYER 1 — GDCR                               │
│ Gateway Domain-Centric Routing               │
│                                              │
│ Exposes the immutable semantic facade        │
│ Applies authentication and access policies   │
│ Loads route metadata from APIM KVM           │
│ Passes context into the DDCR resolver        │
└──────────────────────┬───────────────────────┘
                       │
┌──────────────────────▼───────────────────────┐
│ LAYER 2 — DDCR                               │
│ Domain Driven-Centric Router                 │
│                                              │
│ Parses the semantic URL suffix               │
│ Normalizes the action into one code          │
│ Resolves KVM metadata                        │
│ Builds and sets target.url                   │
└──────────────────────┬───────────────────────┘
                       │
                       ▼
                 SAP CPI / Backend

The script in this post is the DDCR resolver.

It runs as a JavaScript policy in the target request preflow of an SAP API Management proxy. The same JavaScript file can be reused across multiple domain proxies. Each proxy loads a different domain-scoped KVM.

In other words:

The script stays the same. The metadata changes.

That is the core design.

2. What Phantom v12 Actually Does

Phantom v12 receives three required APIM context variables:

Input variable Meaning Example

proxy.pathsuffixThe part of the URL after the APIM proxy base path/orders/create/salesforce
kvm.idinterfaceThe domain route metadata loaded from KVMdcrporderscsalesforceid01:http,...
target.cpi.hostThe configured backend hosthttps://cpi-host.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com

It produces one main routing result:

Output variable / header Meaning Example

target.urlFully resolved backend target URLhttps://cpi-host/http/dcrp/orders/c/id01
dcrp.routing.successRouting statustrue or false
dcrp.routing.errorError reason when routing failsNo route for: orderscsalesforce
x-dcrp-keyMatched KVM keydcrporderscsalesforceid01
x-dcrp-adapterAdapter profile from KVM valuehttp
x-dcrp-processOptional process prefix extracted from KVM keyempty, finance, o2c, etc.
x-idinterfaceRoute ID extracted from key suffix01

If dcrp.routing.success is false, an upstream APIM fault rule can return a controlled error, usually HTTP 404 or 400 depending on the governance rule.

The backend should not be called when the route is not registered.

3. The Contract: What Is Frozen in Phantom v12

Phantom v12 has a specific reference contract.

Frozen in the v12 reference implementation

KVM key format:
  dcrp<process><entity><actioncode><vendor>id<digits>

KVM value format:
  <adapter>

Path suffix format:
  /<entity>/<action>/<vendor>[/<id>[/<extra>...]]

Target URL format:
  <host>/<adapter>/dcrp[/<process>]/<entity>/<actionCode>[/idNN][/<id>][/<extra>][?querystring]

Example:

KVM entry:
  dcrporderscsalesforceid01:http

Request suffix:
  /orders/create/salesforce

Resolved URL:
  https://cpi-host/http/dcrp/orders/c/id01

Important: in Phantom v12, the KVM value is not the full target URL suffix.

The value is only the adapter profile:

http
cxf

The engine itself builds the deterministic target path:

/<adapter>/dcrp[/<process>]/<entity>/<actionCode>/id<nn>

So this is correct for v12:

dcrporderscsalesforceid01:http

This is not the v12 format:

dcrporderscsalesforceid01:http/dcrp/orders/c/id01
